summary: Cathy O'Neil shares a mathematical wallet she received as a 'bribe' from mathematician Jeffrey Hakim, which features a visual proof of Nicomachus's Theorem. The theorem states that the sum of cubes from 1 to n equals the square of the sum from 1 to n. Rather than using the traditional induction proof, the wallet displays an elegant geometric proof using nested frames of squares. O'Neil walks readers through understanding the proof by computing the area of the figure two different ways—one yielding the sum of cubes, the other yielding the squared sum—demonstrating they must be equal.
key_points: Nicomachus's Theorem (sum of cubes equals squared sum) can be proven visually without induction
The geometric proof works by building frames of increasingly larger squares around a center
Computing the area two ways—by quarter-square dimensions versus by counting colored squares—yields both sides of the identity
The pattern generalizes because the dimensions align: (n+1) boxes of length n equals n boxes of length (n+1)
Visual proofs can be more illuminating than algebraic ones for certain identities
money_quotes: It's all in the name of education and fun with mathematics.
This kind of identity is kind of made for induction, no? Don't you think? Well Jeffrey Hakim had an even better idea.
It took me a couple of minutes to see why this is a proof.
If you can, just go ahead and pat yourself on your back.
That's why I got the wallet!
key_insight: A beautiful visual proof can replace algebraic induction by showing that computing the same area two different ways yields both sides of a mathematical identity.
